steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question: the blank must follow logically from both studies taken together. Ellis and Chen found liberalization did not lead to enduring increases in investment. Rao found investment increased significantly in the three years after liberalization. The correct answer must reconcile both findings — a short-term rise that does not last.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "investment growth is likely to be more consistent in countries that liberalize than in countries that do not.": Neither study compares liberalized countries to non-liberalized countries. Ellis and Chen explicitly found increases were not enduring, which contradicts "consistent." ✗
  • "companies typically do not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurs.": Rao found significant increases within three years — not after three years. This misreads the timeline and contradicts Rao's finding. ✗
  • "economists' expectations about the effect of liberalization on investment were largely correct.": The passage does not describe what economists expected. No prior expectation is stated for us to compare the results against. ✗
  • "liberalization may provide a boost to investment that fades over time.": "A boost" captures Rao's finding of significant increases in the first three years. "Fades over time" captures Ellis and Chen's finding that increases were not enduring. Both findings are accounted for, with no unsupported claims added. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
